question: [File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/PollingTopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: poll()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `topicMessageRepository.findByFilter(newFilter)` call in `poll()` — which uses a JPA criteria query that orders by `consensusTimestamp` ascending — such that if two messages have the same `consensusTimestamp` (a condition possible if the database allows duplicate timestamps), the ordering is non-deterministic, causing the `last` cursor to be set to an arbitrary one of the duplicates and potentially causing the other to be skipped in subsequent polls, creating a gap in Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/PollingTopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: poll()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of `payerAccountId` validation in `poll()` — `TopicMessage.getPayerAccountId()` is returned from the database without verifying it corresponds to a real account — such that a tampered message with a fabricated `payerAccountId` is delivered to the subscriber, causing the subscriber to attribute a Hashgraph message to a non-existent or wrong account, falsifying the transaction provenance in the delivered history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/PollingTopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: listen()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `RepeatSpec.times(Long.MAX_VALUE).withFixedDelay(interval).withScheduler(scheduler)` configuration in `listen()` to keep a subscription alive for an effectively infinite duration, and by doing so, receive any retroactively tampered messages that are inserted into the `topic_message` table at timestamps after the current cursor position, since the listener will continue polling and delivering new messages indefinitely without re-validating the integrity of the overall history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/PollingTopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: poll()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `poll()` constructs `newFilter` using `filter.toBuilder().limit(pageSize).startTime(startTime).build()` which does not set `endTime` from the original filter when `filter.getEndTime()` is null, causing the JPA query in `TopicMessageRepositoryCustomImpl.findByFilter()` to omit the `endTime` predicate entirely, and by subscribing without an `endTime`, receive an unbounded stream of all future tampered messages inserted into the database?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/PollingTopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: poll()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of `chunkNum`/`chunkTotal` consistency validation in `poll()` — `TopicMessage.getChunkNum()` and `getChunkTotal()` are returned from the database without verifying that all chunks of a fragmented message are present and contiguous — such that a tampered partial chunk sequence is delivered to the subscriber, causing the subscriber to reconstruct a falsified multi-chunk Hashgraph message?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/PollingTopicListener.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: poll()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `topicMessageRepository.findByFilter(newFilter)` call in `poll()` where `TopicMessageRepositoryCustomImpl` executes `entityManager.createNativeQuery(TOPIC_MESSAGES_BY_ID_QUERY_HINT).executeUpdate()` as a side effect when `limit != 1`, causing a write operation (`set local random_page_cost = 0`) within a `@Transactional(readOnly = true)` transaction, and if this hint manipulation affects query planning for concurrent tampered-message queries, alter the order in which tampered messages are returned?
